Review Title: KFC and Uber make it impossible to complain
Review Text:
I ordered a mighty bucket meal for one to be delivered.
I currently have vertigo so am unable to drive.
My first attempt to pay by card did not allow me to return to the KFC form to confirm so I tried again.
When the delivery came there were 2 bucket meals .
Total cost of £19.96 x2. That includes 2 delivery charges to the same address at the same time. I told the Uber delivery agent I had ordered only one. I didnt want 2. He helped me download app to check order. He said he couldnt take it away because i had 2 PINs . He left.
I ate one meal.
There was no way to find a number to ring the East Prescot Road branch in Liverpool to resolve the matter. The app advised contacting an  Uber number on the order.
I rang this. I spoke to somebody in a far eastern country. They said they could not provide a refund and advised I contact KFC.
The only way left was by a chat app.
I have left my complaint but will anybody get back to me?
Ill have to throw this food away now which is a terrible shame.
I cannot afford to but 2 meals like this.
The mistake was not mine. I followed instructions but the system duplicated my order.
Nobody was available to resolve my issue.
That is the very last time I use KFC.

Review Text:
The order placed through your platform was for 8 Hot Wings, yet only 6 were delivered. This means the goods supplied were incomplete and not what I paid for. Your decision to refuse a refund or any corrective action is unacceptable.

Under Irish consumer law, including the Sale of Goods and Supply of Services Act 1980 and the Consumer Rights Act 2022, consumers are entitled to receive the goods they paid for. Where goods are missing or incorrectly supplied, the trader is required to provide an appropriate remedy such as a refund or replacement.

If this matter is not resolved promptly, I will proceed with escalating the issue. This will include submitting a formal complaint to the Competition and Consumer Protection Commission (CCPC) and initiating a chargeback through my bank for goods paid for but not supplied.

In addition, I intend to share my experience publicly through reviews and with others I interact with professionally. I work in retail and speak with a large number of customers and people daily, and I will be open about this experience and the outcome of this matter.

I have been a Just Eat customer for over 10 years, which makes this response particularly disappointing. I would strongly encourage you to review this matter again and resolve it appropriately.

Review Title: Potentially racist unwelcoming staff at Exeter sidwell kfc.
Review Text:
I recently visited the KFC on Sidwell Street in Exeter to try the new Honey Drip chicken burger. Unfortunately, the food itself was disappointing — it had no spice whatsoever and cost significantly more than the advertised price.

However, the bigger issue was the service. On that visit, the staff member serving me looked me up and down and then accused me of trying to pay with fake money. She took the cash away to check with her manager, who confirmed that it was real. No apology was offered.

When I returned another day, I was served by the same member of staff. Before ordering, I asked politely if I could use the toilet as I was desperate. I was told that the toilets were for customers only. I explained that I was a customer, but instead of resolving the situation, I was told I could speak to the manager. At that point I chose to leave and go somewhere more welcoming.

The repeated attitude and treatment made me feel judged based on my appearance, and honestly came across as stereotyping at best. It left me feeling uncomfortable and unwelcome, which is not how anyone should feel when visiting a fast-food restaurant.

Very disappointing experience overall — both in terms of food quality and customer service.

Review Text:
Shockingly Poor Customer Service and Refusal to Honour Basic Consumer Rights

I’m genuinely disappointed with how KFC has handled my complaint. I paid £25 for an order that wasn’t provided to an acceptable standard. Instead of issuing a refund, KFC gave me a £15 voucher as a goodwill gesture. I made it clear from the very beginning that this voucher was not accepted as a resolution.

Despite repeatedly explaining this, KFC is now refusing to refund me and is claiming that because I used the voucher, the matter is “resolved.” A goodwill voucher does not override statutory rights, does not settle a complaint, and certainly does not replace the money I originally paid.

Every response I received ignored the key point: my rights remain in place regardless of whether a voucher was issued or used. The handling of this complaint has been dismissive, incorrect, and completely out of line with consumer protection law.

I have now passed all correspondence to Trading Standards, and if necessary, I will be taking this to Small Claims Court. It’s not about the £25 — it’s about a company refusing to acknowledge basic consumer rights and trying to close a complaint on flawed reasoning.

Very poor experience, and I won’t be returning until KFC takes customer rights seriously.

Halifax Hailey Hill
Author: Cain Waterhouse
